# Making Architecture Tools Usable by AI {#sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"We shape our tools and thereafter they shape us."*
>
> — John Culkin, *Saturday Review* (1967)
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** John Culkin, an influential American media scholar, observed that tools reshape how people work. An architecture tool also shapes what an AI method can change and what an architect can observe. Those limits must be explicit when the method acts through the tool.
:::


```{=latex}
\abstract*{An AI method needs more than permission to call an architecture tool. The tool path must expose legal actions, returned observations, cost, provenance, constraints, and four distinct tool-level outcomes. This chapter develops that environment contract across ISA boundaries, compiler IRs, memory models, simulator APIs, profilers, RTL flows, EDA handoffs, runtimes, benchmarks, and telemetry systems.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
What must an architecture environment expose so AI can take legal actions
through real tools and another architect can inspect the meaning, cost,
fidelity, and execution status of each returned observation?
:::

An explicit design representation does not make a tool flow usable. Consider
an AI method evaluating a larger L2 cache with gem5, a full-system architecture
simulator [@BinkertEtAl2011Gem5]. The method needs more than a command that
starts the simulator. It needs to know which
cache parameters it may change, which combinations are legal, which workload
and warm-up rules are fixed, how long a run costs, what warnings mean, and
which outputs indicate a valid observation, a broken tool path, or an
unsupported request. Another architect needs the same information to
understand what happened during the run.

An experienced architect may carry much of this knowledge without writing it
down. An AI method cannot rely on that private knowledge. A *tool* performs an
operation. A *wrapper* translates a permitted design action into a tool call,
then translates the result into recorded observations and outcome statuses.
Neither one alone states the fixed context, legal actions, or meaning of the
returned values.

> **Architecture environment.** An architecture environment is a tool flow
> governed by an explicit contract. That contract specifies the legal actions
> and fixed context, the meaning, cost, and fidelity of returned observations,
> the required provenance and integrity information, and distinct tool-level
> outcomes.

The bounded study covers the architecture question, comparison, and intended
decision. Its *environment contract* covers the tool flow. A *harness* is the
maintained runnable implementation of that contract, including workloads,
versions, scripts, provenance, raw outputs, and outcome records. These fields
let a method choose legal actions and let another architect interpret the
returned observations. Whether those observations support an architectural
claim is a separate question developed in @sec-feedback-verification-trust.

A scoped consequence model and an architecture environment answer different
questions. The model predicts or constrains what may happen after an action.
The environment governs the actual tool interaction and records what happens.
A simulator, surrogate, or rule set can contribute to both. The architect must
identify whether each output is a prediction, a constraint, or an observation
from the tool path before passing it to later evidence analysis.

For the L2 example, the contract would name the cache fields that may change,
the fixed workload and warm-up rules, the units and aggregation of each
reported metric, and the cost of a run. It would also distinguish an invalid
configuration, a valid observation that reports a declared limit violation, a
failed tool path, and an observation outside the simulator's declared scope.
The wrapper applies those rules when it calls gem5. The harness retains the
inputs, tool version, raw results, and outcome status. The AI method can then
see what it may request, and another architect can tell what the returned miss
count means.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can turn a tool path into a documented environment
contract. That means you can:

- **Turn** a tool into an environment with specified actions, observations, costs, and four tool-level outcomes;
- **Interpret** a result from its units, aggregation, normalization, uncertainty, scope, and source;
- **Explain** why environment contracts make AI-method comparisons meaningful;
- **Distinguish** invalid actions, valid observations including tool-level constraint failures, broken tool paths, and unsupported observations;
- **Detect** stale configurations, unsupported states, and integrity failures before they corrupt a comparison;
- **Maintain** a comparable harness as workloads, tools, and action schemas change.
:::

## A Tool Call Is Not Yet an Architecture Environment

Computer architects have always used tools to reason quantitatively about
systems. Simulators, analytic models, profilers, compilers, RTL generators,
EDA flows, and measurement systems make design spaces tractable. They also
shape the questions that can be asked. A simulator with a particular memory
model makes some cache questions natural and others awkward. A compiler pass
that provides one schedule representation and hides another constrains what an
optimizer can change. An EDA flow that returns timing and power after hours of
work makes each observation expensive. A runtime telemetry system that reports
aggregate utilization but not per-request interference leaves that interference
outside the observable interface.
When an architect operates the tools directly, some of these limits can remain
tacit. An AI method cannot recover a hidden constraint, cost, warning meaning,
or unsupported state from a scalar score. The environment must make those
conditions available before the method acts.

Calling these conditions tool limitations understates their effect. They
determine what an architect can observe through the tool path. The classic
quantitative tradition in computer
architecture shifted the field from intuition to rigorous simulation and
measurement [@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach]. Architecture 2.0
extends that discipline to the measurement interface itself. Inputs, outputs,
assumptions, and unsupported cases all affect what a result means.

An AI method can act only on the interface state it receives. An architect can
sometimes infer that a simulator result is
out of distribution, that a benchmark run used a stale configuration, or that
a reported improvement is not meaningful because the compiler changed. A
method cannot use those facts unless the wrapper exposes them. It needs enough
state to choose a useful action and enough constraints to recognize an invalid
one.

Naming gem5, FireSim, or a commercial EDA tool is not enough. Another architect
also needs to know which actions the wrapper permits, what the tool returns,
which states are invalid, and which runs can be repeated. Those details
determine whether two methods actually used the same environment.

## Architecture Environment Inputs, Actions, and Observations

The contract connects a permitted design action to a tool result whose meaning
is explicit. It names the architecture question and workload, the state that
may change, the state that must remain fixed, the observations the tool can
return, the cost and fidelity of obtaining them, and the possible outcomes of
the call. @tbl-environment-contract collects those requirements.

| **Field** | **What it must expose** | **Why another architect needs it** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Workload and fixed context** | Architecture question, inputs, traces, benchmark and software versions, operating conditions, and choices held constant. | Shows which behavior the result describes and whether two runs used the same context. |
| **Action schema** | Parameters, edits, configurations, generated artifacts, or commands an AI method may propose. | Defines exactly what the method can change. |
| **Constraints and checks** | Type and range checks, legal combinations, software-visible contracts, physical limits, and checks applied before and after a tool call. | Separates an invalid action from a valid observation that reports a declared tool-level constraint failure. |
| **Observation schema** | Metrics, traces, logs, reports, errors, status values, and artifacts returned by the tool path. | Identifies the raw result and the source from which it came. |
| **Observation meaning** | Units, aggregation rule, denominator, normalization, preferred direction, uncertainty or run-to-run variation, and valid scope. | Lets a reviewer interpret a value rather than infer what a metric name means. |
| **Evaluation cost and fidelity** | Wall time, compute, license or hardware use, engineering effort, model stage, determinism, and practical sample limit. | Shows which comparisons are affordable and which observations can be compared. |
| **Provenance and integrity** | Tool, model, prompt, dependency, dataset, host, seed, configuration, artifact identifiers, hashes, and signatures when available. | Shows which inputs and software produced the result and whether the tool source changed. |
| **Tool-level outcome** | Invalid action before execution, valid observation returned, execution or tool-path failure, and unsupported or out-of-scope observation. | Preserves what happened during the call without treating a tool status as an architecture decision. |

: **An environment contract defines tool use before execution.** It tells an AI method what it may request and lets another architect interpret the returned result. It does not decide which architectural claim the result supports. {#tbl-environment-contract tbl-colwidths="[22,38,30]"}

A metric name alone is not enough. If gem5 reports cache misses, the contract
must say whether the value is a count or rate, which requests form the
denominator, whether warm-up traffic was removed, whether results are per core
or aggregated, which direction is preferred, and how much the value varies
across repeated runs. Without that information, an optimizer can rank numbers
that another architect cannot interpret.

The outcome status also matters. Four tool-level outcomes require different
records.

- **Invalid action before execution:** The proposed action violates the schema or a
  condition checked before the tool runs. The wrapper declines the request
  without treating it as an evaluated design result.
- **Valid observation returned:** The tool path completes and returns an
  observation within its declared scope. A timing violation, failed assertion,
  congestion warning, or other declared tool-level constraint failure is still
  a valid observation, not an invalid action or broken tool path.
- **Execution or tool-path failure:** The setup, wrapper, harness, license, or tool fails to
  produce a valid observation. The harness records the failure and its cause
  rather than assigning the result to the candidate by default.
- **Unsupported or out-of-scope observation:** The tool returns an output that
  its contract does not define for the requested state, configuration, or
  question. The harness preserves the output without relabeling it as a valid
  measurement.

These distinctions describe what happened in the tool path. A separate study
rule may route an invalid request back to the method, send an execution failure
to the environment maintainer, or send an unsupported observation to an
architect. The status itself does not reject an architecture candidate, decide
what the observation proves, or authorize the next design decision. Those
judgments belong to @sec-feedback-verification-trust and
@sec-what-architect-owns.

A completed SCALE-Sim comparison provides a concrete example. Its action schema
admitted only legal array geometries. SCALE-Sim returned cycle count and average
layer utilization for accepted inputs, and the harness retained the raw
outputs, status, elapsed time, simulator configuration, and candidate source.
The fixed meanings and declared simulator-run budget let another architect
interpret the resulting tie. That comparison still does not equalize prior
knowledge, method-development effort, or human effort. The simulator returned
observations; it did not decide which architectural claim those observations
supported.

## Wrappers and Harnesses Record Tool Outcomes

The wrapper and harness make the environment contract executable.
@fig-environment-tool-interface separates the information supplied before a
tool call from the observations and status returned afterward. The center shows
the wrapper checking, calling, and parsing while the harness records the run.

![**A tool becomes an environment when its interface is specified.** The contract fixes permitted actions and observation meaning before execution. The wrapper and harness then keep invalid actions, valid observations including declared constraint failures, execution failures, and unsupported observations distinct while preserving the raw output and provenance of every path.](images/F6-environment-tool-interface){#fig-environment-tool-interface width="100%" fig-alt="Diagram of an architecture question and workload, action schema, constraints, and metric definitions entering an environment boundary. A wrapper checks the request, calls the tool, and parses the observation and status; a harness records the run and provenance. Separate branches represent invalid action before execution, valid observation returned, execution or tool-path failure, and unsupported or out-of-scope observation."}

On the left of @fig-environment-tool-interface, the architecture question and
workload establish the inputs, version, and fixed context. The action schema
names the knobs, edits, or commands an AI method may propose. The constraints
and metric definitions state which requests are legal and how returned values
should be read.

Inside the environment boundary, the wrapper checks the request, invokes the
tool, and parses its observations and status. The harness records the run and
its provenance. An invalid action stops before execution. A completed tool run
returns a valid observation, including any declared constraint failure reported
by the tool. A broken setup, harness, license, or tool produces an execution or
tool-path failure instead. An output whose meaning falls outside the contract
is recorded as unsupported or out of scope.

The right side separates raw tool output, such as metrics, logs, and reports,
from the run record that preserves those outputs, their meaning, cost,
fidelity, provenance, and status. The figure also preserves every non-success
path. An optimizer may receive one scalar objective, while another architect
can still inspect the underlying cycle counts, warnings, configuration, and
tool version. The record documents the tool call; later analysis determines
what to do with the observation. Two projects can use the same
simulator while defining different architecture environments because their
actions, workloads, constraints, and observation meanings differ.

The harness should retain the underlying observations and cost data even when a
method receives only a scalar objective. Otherwise, the method may exploit a
simulator bug or omitted cost instead of improving the design.

## Interfaces Differ Across Architecture Tools

A tool's interface makes its action and observation schema concrete.
Architecture is often described through boundaries between hardware and
software. For an AI system to work through those boundaries, the architect
must spell out what each interface permits, what observations it returns, and
which failures it can detect. This applies to ISA boundaries, compiler IRs,
memory models, runtime APIs, simulator interfaces, benchmark harnesses, EDA
handoffs, and telemetry schemas. When the study depends on coherence,
scheduling, memory semantics, or physical constraints, the method should act
through the interface or schema that defines those semantics. Unstructured C++
or RTL becomes a review risk when it bypasses those modular interfaces, not
because it is invalid by default.

Tool interfaces belong in the architecture argument rather than in
an appendix. A generator that emits a schedule must use the schedule language.
A search method that changes memory hierarchy parameters must know which
combinations the simulator accepts and which violate a software-visible
contract. A critic that reads a synthesis report must distinguish a fatal
warning from an informational one and know when a higher-fidelity check is
required. The interface connects an AI method's permitted actions to the
architectural rules that govern them.

MLIR, a compiler infrastructure built around reusable and extensible
intermediate representations, illustrates why a compiler interface must expose
the transformations and target-specific semantics available to a method rather
than present compilation as one generic action [@LattnerEtAl2020MLIR]. A
simulator, RTL flow, EDA stage, or runtime exposes a different boundary and
therefore needs a different action and observation schema.

An AI-assisted architecture comparison may depend on several interfaces
(@tbl-architecture-interface-boundaries). Each interface defines what the
method may do and how an architect should read the tool output. If either
remains implicit, an optimizer may change something the architect meant to
hold fixed or assign a meaning to the tool output that the interface never
defined.

| **Interface** | **What the AI method may do** | **What the tool returns and a reviewer can check** | **Risk if hidden** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| ISA and vector contract | Instructions, registers, vector length, exceptions, privilege, and binary compatibility. | Correct execution, portability, software-visible behavior, and compatibility tests. | An AI method proposes a microarchitecture that software cannot legally target. |
| Compiler IR and schedule representation | Lowering choices, tiling, fusion, layout, vectorization, and target-specific code generation. | Compiler success, generated code, performance counters, and optimization provenance. | Performance is attributed to hardware while the software contract changed. |
| Memory and coherence model | Ordering, sharing, cacheability, consistency, DMA, and synchronization assumptions. | Correctness tests, contention behavior, latency, bandwidth, and race or ordering failures. | A candidate looks fast because it violated the program's memory assumptions. |
| Accelerator or runtime API | Invocation, data movement, synchronization, library calls, queues, and resource ownership. | End-to-end latency, overhead, utilization, portability, and software integration cost. | Specialized hardware is efficient in isolation but unusable in the system. |
| Simulator or environment API | Legal parameters, workload inputs, observations, errors, seeds, and fidelity levels. | Run observations, errors, seeds, invalid-action records, elapsed cost, and the metadata needed to check comparability. | The method optimizes simulator quirks or incomparable configurations. |
| EDA handoff and constraints | RTL, clocks, floorplan hints, timing constraints, power intent, physical limits, and signoff checks. | Timing, area, power, congestion, rule violations, warnings, and closure status. | A candidate survives architectural simulation but encounters a physical constraint the simulator did not model. |
| Benchmark harness | Inputs, versions, metrics, splits, data-leakage rules, and submission constraints. | Scores, per-case results, coverage metadata, and the version and split information needed to check leakage or repeat the run. | An AI method overfits a stale or leaky benchmark slice. |
| Telemetry and deployment schema | Live workload mix, service-level objectives (SLOs), counters, interference, rollout state, and drift signals. | Field behavior, regressions, rollback triggers, collection gaps, and changing workload context. | Aggregate telemetry hides whether a change came from hardware, software, workload mix, or deployment state. |

: **Architecture interfaces define what a method can do and what reviewers can inspect.** Each interface states what an AI method may change, what the tool returns, and which tool-level failures or unsupported states it can report. {#tbl-architecture-interface-boundaries tbl-colwidths="[20,25,25,20]"}

The interfaces in @tbl-architecture-interface-boundaries rarely operate alone.
Suppose an AI method proposes a wider vector unit. The ISA contract determines
whether software can target it, the compiler and runtime determine whether a
workload uses it, the simulator estimates its performance, and the EDA handoff
tests whether the added datapaths can meet timing and power limits. A gain at
one interface does not erase a constraint reported at another. The environment
must keep the actions and observations from those tool stages connected so
another architect can tell which configuration produced each result.

### EDA Handoffs and Closed Flows

The EDA handoff row is worth making concrete because its wrapper must translate
between proposed changes and several tool stages. Three checks occur at
different times. Before execution, schema checks ask whether the parameters,
clocks, and interfaces are well formed. After execution, physical checks ask
whether the candidate is routable and can meet timing, congestion, power, and
IR-drop limits. Separately, lifecycle checks ask whether the harness still
tests the intended architecture question after tools, workloads, or interfaces
change. A late physical failure belongs to the evaluated candidate; it is not
an invalid action or a broken tool path. It is a valid observation of a
tool-level constraint failure.

Wrapping a commercial flow requires more than a thin API. The wrapper has to
emit tool-specific scripts, parse unstructured reports, survive
timeouts and run-to-run nondeterminism. The architect defines the fidelity label
for each tool stage, and the wrapper records it with the run. The tool itself
emits metrics, warnings, and logs, not a judgment about what those results can
support. To stay auditable, the provenance record must include more than a bare
number. It records the tool and host version, thread count, license state,
run-to-run variance, warnings and waivers, and a hash of the report the number
came from. Two "identical" runs that differ on any of these should not be treated
as comparable until the architect shows that the difference does not affect the
result. A wrapper that hides the difference can make incompatible runs look
like a fair comparison.

Commercial tool and foundry constraints also limit what Architecture 2.0 should
try to standardize. The field does not need a universal vendor API or an open
substitute for every foundry rule. It needs a stable way for reviewers to see
what changed, what was observed, what stayed confidential, and which tool-level
status was returned, while leaving proprietary implementation details protected
(@tbl-standardizable-proprietary-contract).

| **Contract layer** | **Can be standardized** | **Usually remains proprietary** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Action schema | Task identifiers, legal edit classes, invalid-action categories, and stage labels. | Vendor command syntax, internal scripts, and product-specific flow recipes. |
| Observation schema | Metric names, fidelity labels, report hashes, warning classes, and failure categories. | Full report bodies, foundry rule details, and confidential workload traces. |
| Run record | Tool/version hashes, seed records, queue or license state, elapsed time, and artifact pointers. | License-server behavior, internal tool telemetry, and nonpublic debug artifacts. |
| Tool outcome | Four common status categories, affected artifact, raw-output pointer, and retry eligibility. | Internal warning semantics, protected failure details, and vendor-specific recovery procedures. |
| Disclosure boundary | Redaction reason, disclosure tier, artifact hash or signature, and which observations remain unavailable. | Raw PDK contents, proprietary RTL, private baselines, and customer data. |

: **Run records for closed tool flows.** Commercial EDA and foundry flows can use common action, observation, provenance, outcome, and disclosure fields without exposing vendor implementations, foundry rules, or protected design state. {#tbl-standardizable-proprietary-contract tbl-colwidths="[18,34,38]"}

Across all five layers, schemas and run records can be standardized while
tool commands, protected data, and implementation details remain private.

### Multi-Fidelity Tool Chains

Architecture tools expose these interfaces at different fidelity levels.
Architecture 2.0 does not require one canonical simulator or vendor
flow. The architect must identify the tool path, the observations it can
return, their cost and fidelity, and the states each path does not support
(@tbl-environment-instances).

The gem5 and FireSim rows below use published systems papers. The Verilator row
uses the project's first-party documentation for the stated interface and
generated-model features; it is not an independent performance comparison.

| **Environment instance** | **Permitted actions** | **Returned observations and records** | **What the environment must preserve** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Cycle-level or full-system simulation (e.g., gem5) [@BinkertEtAl2011Gem5] | Core, cache, memory-system, ISA, and workload configuration. | Statistics, traces, timing-model behavior, simulator warnings, and scoped performance comparisons. | Configuration state, workload rules, warnings, elapsed cost, and unsupported states. |
| Compiled RTL simulation (e.g., Verilator) [@Veripool2026Verilator] | RTL modules, test benches, assertions, generated C++/SystemC models, and debug hooks. | Functional behavior, waveforms, debug logs, assertion failures, and implementation-adjacent traces. | Build state, test bench, assertions, raw logs, elapsed cost, and execution failures. |
| FPGA-accelerated simulation (e.g., FireSim) [@KarandikarEtAl2018FireSim] | RTL target, workload image, network model, FPGA mapping, and runtime configuration. | Cycle-exact observations, workload-scale behavior, instrumented counters, and emulation results. | Target and host configurations, workload image, instrumentation, elapsed cost, and unsupported mappings. |
| Synthesis, place-and-route, and signoff flow [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement; @SemiconductorIndustryAssociation2026ChipDesign; @BauerEtAl2020SemiconductorDesignManufacturing] | RTL, constraints, floorplan hints, clocks, power intent, libraries, and process assumptions. | Area, timing, power, congestion, rule violations, waived warnings, and closure failures. | Tool stage, libraries, constraints, reports, warnings, runtime, and tool-level outcome. |

: **Tool-specific environment records.** For each comparison, record the simulator, RTL flow, emulation path, EDA stage, or deployment harness because each provides different actions, observations, costs, and outcome paths. {#tbl-environment-instances tbl-colwidths="[22,24,26,18]"}

For review, the tool name matters less than the record of permitted edits,
workloads, observations, outcome statuses, and configuration state.

In practice these environments are rarely used in isolation. Open frameworks
integrate them. Chipyard, an open-source framework for generating and evaluating
RISC-V systems-on-chip, connects a configuration system, Chisel-based RTL
generators such as the Rocket and BOOM cores and the Hwacha vector unit, and a
FIRRTL compiler path.
It ties these to simulation, synthesis, and FPGA-emulation backends, so each
evaluation begins from the same configuration (@fig-chipyard-framework)
[@chipyard]. An optimizer edits a validated configuration. Simulation,
synthesis, and FPGA emulation then evaluate that configuration, while the
harness records the output and outcome status from each backend.

![**An integrated environment ties the backends together:** A reproducible SoC generation framework connects a configuration system, RTL generators, and a compiler to simulation, synthesis, and FPGA-emulation backends, so one configuration reaches every evaluation path.](images/F6b-chipyard-framework){#fig-chipyard-framework width="100%" fig-alt="Flow diagram in which a configuration system feeds Chisel RTL generators (Rocket, BOOM, Hwacha) and a FIRRTL compiler, which fan out to RTL simulation, VLSI synthesis, and FPGA emulation backends."}

Read @fig-chipyard-framework from the configuration on the left. Its parameters
drive the Chisel generators and FIRRTL compiler, which produce the RTL consumed
by three evaluation paths. RTL simulation can expose functional and
microarchitectural behavior, the VLSI flow can expose implementation limits,
and FPGA emulation can run larger workloads. Sending one validated
configuration to all three paths keeps the candidate identity stable while the
returned observations and findings change. The framework supplies that
connection, but an environment contract must still record the workload,
backend versions, constraints, costs, warnings, and outcome status for each
path.

The three paths also show why an integrated framework does not collapse
fidelity differences. Post-synthesis timing, for
example, differs from post-route timing because routing congestion, IR drop,
clocking, and later checks are not yet available. The contract records the tool
stage with every observation so a synthesis result cannot be mistaken for a
post-route result. If the later path reports a timing or congestion violation,
that is a valid observation from that stage. Whether it changes the
architectural conclusion is developed in @sec-feedback-verification-trust.

Physical-design environments make this staged contract particularly clear. A
wrapper should not report "EDA feedback" as if every tool stage returned the
same kind of result. Logic synthesis, placement, routing, and signoff expose
different observations and failure modes. The contract must state the stage,
raw output, and tool-level status for each return (@tbl-eda-stage-contract).

| **EDA stage** | **Observation returned** | **Problem it can reveal** | **Tool-level outcome to preserve** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Logic synthesis | Mapped gates, area estimates, timing estimates, constraint warnings, and early power estimates. | Nonsynthesizable RTL, impossible constraints, and early area or timing problems. | A completed report, including declared constraint failures, is a valid observation; failure to produce the report is a tool-path failure. |
| Floorplanning and placement | Cell locations, utilization, congestion hints, timing pressure, and macro or memory placement effects. | Floorplans that create severe congestion, timing pressure, or integration problems. | Congestion or timing violations are valid observations from this stage, while an unsupported floorplan state remains out of scope. |
| Clock, routing, and power closure | Routed timing, clock behavior, congestion, design-rule checks, power integrity, and closure failures. | Physical effects absent from earlier tool stages. | Preserve each reported violation and warning separately from a timeout, license failure, or broken run. |
| Signoff analysis | Signoff reports, warning classes, violations, analysis conditions, and waived tool messages. | Conditions that remain unresolved at the end of the declared physical tool path. | Record the report and tool outcome without treating the tool status as an architecture decision. |

: **EDA stages return different findings.** From logic synthesis through
signoff, each physical-design stage can reveal different candidate problems.
The environment records the stage, raw output, and tool-level outcome before a
later review determines how the finding affects the claim. {#tbl-eda-stage-contract tbl-colwidths="[18,26,25,21]"}

First-party descriptions from Synopsys and Cadence report commercial systems
that search physical-implementation choices and evaluate them through their
respective tool flows [@Synopsys2023DSOai; @Cadence2021Cerebrus]. Those vendor
sources establish the stated product features, not an independent comparison.
An environment around either flow must define the legal directives, preserve
the reports and tool versions, and distinguish a reported closure violation
from a broken execution path or unsupported configuration.

## ArchGym as a Worked Example

ArchGym, a project from the author's group, shows these requirements in the
architecture domain. It organizes bounded architecture tasks as gym-style
environments with defined actions and observations
[@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym]. The ArchGym paper describes this interface as based
on OpenAI Gym and shows memory-controller and accelerator environments behind
a common method interface. The Architecture 2.0 gymnasium essay makes the
related case for shared datasets, tasks, tools, and evaluation environments
[@ReddiYazdanbakhsh2023Gymnasium].

ArchGym illustrates how a shared environment changes the research question.
Instead of asking whether one optimizer beat another under a private script, the
community can ask which task was defined, which action space was exposed, what
feedback was available, what workloads were used, and which tools or methods
were compared under the same conditions. @fig-ch05-archgym-equal-budget shows
why the simulator-sample budget has to be part of that comparison. It places
five search families inside the same DRAM memory-controller environment and
varies only how many simulator evaluations each family can spend
[@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym]. Inspect how the apparent ranking changes from the
smallest budgets to the largest rather than looking for one overall winner.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch05-archgym-equal-budget
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Rankings under equal evaluation budgets.** Mean normalized reward (higher is better) for five search families inside the same ArchGym DRAM memory-controller environment at equal simulator-sample budgets [@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym]. Values and whisker extents are digitized transcriptions of the paper's Figure 7(a), not raw result files. The discussion surrounding the plot does not state the normalization reference, aggregation population, or whisker statistic. Intervals overlap across families at every budget, and reinforcement learning has no bar at the two smallest budgets in the source. Read the plot as a budget-dependent comparison, not a significance test or a precise ranking.
#| out-width: "88%"
#| fig-alt: "Grouped bar chart with whiskers showing digitized mean normalized reward for five search families, ACO, BO, GA, RL, and RW, at simulator-sample budgets from one hundred to two hundred fifty thousand in the ArchGym DRAM memory-controller environment. Whisker intervals overlap across families at every budget. Reinforcement learning has no source bar at the two smallest budgets, is near zero at ten thousand samples, and has the highest digitized mean only at the largest budget."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, apply_style

apply_style()

# Source receipt: data/source-receipts/chapter5-archgym-equal-budget.csv
# ArchGym ISCA 2023 [@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym], Fig. 7(a): mean normalized
# reward vs. simulator-sample budget for DRAM memory-controller design
# (DRAMGym, DRAMSys). Means and whisker extents are programmatically
# digitized from the published figure's native-resolution raster (arXiv
# 2306.08888v1 PDF, accessed 2026-07-13; axis-calibration residual <= 0.002
# reward units, reading uncertainty ~ +/-0.003). The paper does not define the
# normalization reference, aggregation population, or statistical meaning of
# its whiskers in the discussion surrounding Figure 7. RL bars are absent in
# the source at the 100 and 1,000-sample budgets and are omitted, not zeroed.
budgets = ["100", "1K", "10K", "100K", "250K"]
agents = [
    ("ACO", COLORS["blue"]),
    ("BO", COLORS["orange"]),
    ("GA", COLORS["green"]),
    ("RL", COLORS["red"]),
    ("RW", COLORS["purple"]),
]
# (mean, whisker low, whisker high) per budget; None = not plotted in source
dram = {
    "ACO": [(0.026, 0.017, 0.037), (0.034, 0.024, 0.046), (0.045, 0.033, 0.058),
            (0.030, 0.021, 0.041), (0.051, 0.039, 0.065)],
    "BO": [(0.046, 0.025, 0.064), (0.112, 0.042, 0.182), (0.112, 0.042, 0.182),
           (0.112, 0.042, 0.182), (0.115, 0.048, 0.182)],
    "GA": [(0.043, 0.025, 0.070), (0.083, 0.053, 0.110), (0.098, 0.070, 0.124),
           (0.097, 0.062, 0.124), (0.107, 0.081, 0.125)],
    "RL": [None, None, (0.007, 0.003, 0.013),
           (0.111, 0.062, 0.208), (0.154, 0.134, 0.168)],
    "RW": [(0.027, 0.019, 0.032), (0.027, 0.014, 0.037), (0.132, 0.112, 0.153),
           (0.132, 0.117, 0.147), (0.132, 0.121, 0.142)],
}

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.7, 2.25))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.105, right=0.985, top=0.83, bottom=0.19)

width = 0.15
for k, (name, color) in enumerate(agents):
    xs, ys, err_lo, err_hi = [], [], [], []
    for i, v in enumerate(dram[name]):
        if v is None:
            continue
        xs.append(i + (k - 2) * width)
        ys.append(v[0])
        err_lo.append(v[0] - v[1])
        err_hi.append(v[2] - v[0])
    ax.bar(xs, ys, width=width * 0.9, color=color, zorder=2, label=name)
    ax.errorbar(xs, ys, yerr=[err_lo, err_hi], fmt="none",
                ecolor=COLORS["ink"], elinewidth=0.7, capsize=1.4,
                capthick=0.7, zorder=3)

ax.set_xticks(range(len(budgets)))
ax.set_xticklabels(budgets)
ax.set_xlabel("simulator-sample budget", fontsize=7)
ax.set_ylabel("mean normalized reward", fontsize=7)
ax.set_ylim(0, 0.23)
ax.set_yticks([0.0, 0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20])
ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=6.5, length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
ax.grid(axis="y", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.55)
for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
fig.legend(loc="upper center", ncol=5, fontsize=6.2, frameon=False,
           bbox_to_anchor=(0.53, 1.0), handlelength=1.0, columnspacing=1.2)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

At 10,000 or fewer samples in @fig-ch05-archgym-equal-budget, the paper
describes a random walker as competitive with Bayesian optimization, genetic
algorithms, and ant colony optimization. Reinforcement learning improves
sharply at 100,000 or more samples, while simpler methods remain competitive.
The paper separately reports an interquartile spread of up to 90 percent across
hyperparameter choices in the DRAM task. Those changes mean that an apparent
method advantage can depend on the evaluation budget and tuning effort, not
only on the search family.

The whisker intervals overlap across the figure, and the plotted values were
digitized from the published raster rather than recomputed from raw results.
The figure supports a narrow observation about how reported results change
with sample budget. It does not provide an absolute reward interpretation or a
precise pairwise ranking. That limitation shows why the environment must record
both acquisition budget and observation meaning.

The published ArchGym comparison also shows that a shared action-observation
interface does not remove every experimental degree of freedom.

::: {.callout-war-story title="A shared interface did not fix the comparison budget"}
**The claim.** ArchGym compared five search families inside the same DRAM
memory-controller environment under several simulator-sample budgets
[@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym].

**The gap.** The reported ranking changed with the sample budget, and the paper
reported an interquartile spread of up to 90 percent across hyperparameter
choices in the DRAM task. The discussion surrounding the source plot did not
state the reward normalization reference, aggregation population, or whisker
statistic.

**The lesson.** A common API fixes only part of the environment. Workload,
simulator configuration, sample budget, tuning procedure, and metric definition
must also travel with each run.
:::

ArchGym can be described using the contract fields in
@tbl-environment-contract (@tbl-archgym-mini-contract). The framework supplies
a shared task, action, and simulator interface, but each comparison still has
to define the metric, outcome statuses, dependency and artifact versions, and
the follow-up associated with each outcome. ArchGym does not fill those fields
for every comparison. The architect must define them for the comparison at
hand.

| **Contract field** | **What a reviewable ArchGym use needs** |
| --- | --- |
| **Task** | Search or tune a bounded architecture design space under a declared workload and metric. |
| **Action schema** | Architecture parameters, simulator knobs, or mapping choices the automated method is allowed to change. |
| **Observation and reward** | Simulator metrics, their units and scope, and the user-defined reward derived from them. For the Figure 7 comparison, the published plot reports mean normalized reward but does not state the normalization reference, aggregation population, or whisker statistic. |
| **Workload and tool path** | Benchmark inputs, simulator configuration, versioned evaluation scripts, and artifact identifiers. |
| **Tool-level outcome** | Separate status values for an invalid action before execution, a valid observation returned including a declared constraint failure, an execution or tool-path failure, and an unsupported or out-of-scope observation. |
| **Evaluation cost** | Approximate turnaround and sample budget for each evaluation regime. |
| **Follow-up** | The retry, repair, maintenance, or review action associated with each status, recorded separately from the status itself. |
| **Limits** | Simulator behaviors, action-space restrictions, and industrial constraints that the environment does not expose. |

: **Mapping ArchGym to the environment contract.** ArchGym supplies the shared task, action, and simulator interface. A reviewable comparison still has to fill in metric meaning, tool outcomes, provenance, acquisition cost, and follow-up actions. {#tbl-archgym-mini-contract tbl-colwidths="[24,66]"}

A single environment can support Bayesian optimization, reinforcement learning,
evolutionary search, random search, or an architect using the environment as an
instrumented assistant. Defining the environment separately from the method
makes those comparisons possible. Choosing which method should act inside that
environment is the subject of @sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization.

ArchGym covers bounded tasks through shared interfaces. Industrial design may
add proprietary physical constraints, confidential workloads, tool-license
behavior, workload drift, failed-run capture, and deployment telemetry. Its
action spaces may also be less regular and its feedback slower than the
environments ArchGym exposes. The architect must check whether the shared
environment still represents the intended architecture problem before
extending a result beyond its stated task.

## Stable Interfaces Let Methods Share a Harness

A wrapper written for one experiment can hide assumptions in its scripts. A
stable interface lets several methods work on the same architecture task
without rebuilding those assumptions for each method. They can then share the
same action schema, observations, workloads, provenance, and tool-outcome rules
without silently changing the environment.

Benchmark governance already provides rules, versions, metrics, submission
formats, and comparison conventions that help a community interpret results
[@MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf]. The shared interface must additionally state
what an AI method may try, which actions are invalid, what feedback means, and
how each tool-level outcome is represented. Sending an unsupported observation
for later review is recorded separately from the observation status.
Publishing a script without that contract leaves the important assumptions
private. A maintained interface must also version and revalidate those
assumptions after the first paper or release.

A maintained harness adds shared workloads, versions, permissions, and run
records around the wrapper. It preserves the task and workload versions,
editable and read-only fields, exact commands and observations, cost and
fidelity, seeds, tool versions, artifacts, and the outcome status. Another
architect can then inspect the command that produced a score together with the
raw observations, warnings, invalid actions, execution failures, and
unsupported returns.

If several AI components share the harness, it also records which represented
state each component read, which action it requested, and which tool return it
received. Stable fields preserve the environment while the method changes.

## Feedback Cost and Fidelity Across Tool Paths

Every observation returned through an environment must carry its cost and
fidelity.
An analytic proxy may return in milliseconds, a cycle-level simulation may take
minutes or hours, and physical implementation or silicon measurements may
require days to months. If these differences remain hidden, a fast method can
overwhelm an expensive tool path or compare measurements that do not mean the
same thing.

Observation sources differ by orders of magnitude in turnaround and in how many
calls an evaluation budget can afford. @fig-fidelity-cost-confidence compares
those sources from analytic estimates through deployment observations. Its
ranges are editorial constructions that show representative ordering, not
measurements reported by one source. Published papers supply the marked
FireSim and physical-design examples. Each run still needs its tool stage and
version, elapsed cost, fidelity, raw observations, and outcome status.

The cost structure is a multi-fidelity problem familiar in computational
science, where many cheaper evaluations are combined with fewer expensive ones
to make optimization affordable
[@PeherstorferWillcoxGunzburger2018Multifidelity]. An architecture environment
must expose the available tool paths and acquisition costs. The method that
chooses among them is developed in
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization.

```{python}
#| label: fig-fidelity-cost-confidence
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Tool paths have different acquisition costs and sample budgets.** The unmarked ranges are editorial constructions that show representative ordering, not measured intervals. Ink diamonds mark published examples. FireSim reports 1,024-node cycle-exact simulation at 3.4 MHz, less than 1,000 times slower than the 3.2 GHz targets it models, while the paper reports 5 to 100 KIPS for single-server software simulators [@KarandikarEtAl2018FireSim]. An updated macro-placement assessment reports 11 hours per design for simulated annealing and 38 to 55 hours for the reinforcement-learning flow, against under 12 hours of expert effort, for one physical-design stage evaluated through a commercial post-route flow [@ChengEtAl2026UpdatedAssessment].
#| out-width: "92%"
#| fig-alt: "Horizontal range plot comparing editorially constructed observation-latency ranges and shrinking sample budgets from analytic estimates to silicon deployment. Ink diamond markers add published examples, a FireSim cycle-exact simulation point at just under one thousand times real time on the cycle-level simulation row, and macro-placement turnaround points from eleven to fifty-five hours per design on the physical-feedback row."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, add_note_box, apply_style, draw_range_rows, row_axis, top_log_axis

rows = [
    {"display_label": "analytic proxy / surrogate", "display_note": "thousands to millions of checks", "low_seconds": 0.001, "high_seconds": 1, "right_label": "screen candidates", "color": COLORS["blue"]},
    {"display_label": "trace / profile / replay", "display_note": "tens to thousands of slices", "low_seconds": 1, "high_seconds": 3600, "right_label": "characterize workload", "color": COLORS["green"]},
    {"display_label": "cycle-level simulation", "display_note": "tens to hundreds of comparisons", "low_seconds": 60, "high_seconds": 86400, "right_label": "compare candidates", "color": COLORS["orange"]},
    {"display_label": "compiler / runtime measurement", "display_note": "many local measurements; target state matters", "low_seconds": 1, "high_seconds": 3600, "right_label": "exercise software path", "color": COLORS["purple"]},
    {"display_label": "RTL / synthesis / physical feedback", "display_note": "handfuls to tens of scarce samples", "low_seconds": 3600, "high_seconds": 1_209_600, "right_label": "implementation observations", "color": COLORS["red"]},
    {"display_label": "prototype / silicon / deployment", "display_note": "few deployment observations plus telemetry", "low_seconds": 604_800, "high_seconds": 31_536_000, "right_label": "deployment observations", "color": COLORS["red"]},
]

apply_style()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.85, 3.75))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.39, right=0.77, top=0.86, bottom=0.16)

row_axis(ax, len(rows))
top_log_axis(
    ax,
    xlim=(5e-4, 7e7),
    xticks=[1e-3, 1, 3600, 604800, 31536000],
    xticklabels=["1 ms", "1 s", "1 h", "1 wk", "1 yr"],
    xlabel="representative observation latency",
)
draw_range_rows(ax, rows, low_key="low_seconds", high_key="high_seconds", label_x=-0.90, right_x=1.03, label_fontsize=6.5, note_fontsize=5.3, right_fontsize=5.9)

# Measured anchors (source receipt:
# data/source-receipts/chapter5-measured-feedback-anchors.csv).
# FireSim ISCA 2018 [@KarandikarEtAl2018FireSim]: 1,024-node cycle-exact
# simulation at 3.4 MHz vs. 3.2 GHz targets, "less than 1,000x slowdown over
# real-time" -> ~941 s to observe one second of target execution; the paper
# also gives per-node 10s-100s MHz and 5-100 KIPS for single-server software
# simulators (rates only, cited in the caption, no latency marker).
# Cheng et al. TCAD 2026 [@ChengEtAl2026UpdatedAssessment], Table IV, CT-Ariane:
# macro placement evaluated through a commercial post-route flow, SA 11.13 h,
# RL 37.89-55.16 h, human expert <12 h per design. Accessed 2026-07-13.
# The Verilator anchor is dropped: its only absolute measured rate is outside
# the bibliography.
firesim_x = 3.2e9 / 3.4e6  # ~941 s per second of target execution
sa_x = 11.13 * 3600
rl_lo_x, rl_hi_x = 37.89 * 3600, 55.16 * 3600
# The RL 38-55 h range is anchored by ink diamonds at both endpoints so every
# measured anchor uses the same diamond vocabulary as the note box.
ax.scatter([firesim_x, sa_x, rl_lo_x, rl_hi_x], [2, 4, 4, 4], marker="D",
           s=11, color=COLORS["ink"], zorder=4)
ax.text(firesim_x, 2.34,
        "FireSim, 1,024 nodes, cycle-exact:\n<1,000$\\times$ real time",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=5.3, color=COLORS["muted"],
        linespacing=1.15, clip_on=False)
ax.text(3.2e4, 4.34,
        "macro placement, measured:\nSA 11 h, RL 38$-$55 h, expert <12 h",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=5.3, color=COLORS["muted"],
        linespacing=1.15, clip_on=False)

add_note_box(fig, "Unmarked ranges are editorial constructions; ink diamonds mark published examples; every run still needs a stage, version, cost, and tool outcome.", xywh=(0.13, 0.035, 0.74, 0.085), fontsize=5.5)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

Read @fig-fidelity-cost-confidence from top to bottom. Cheap proxies and traces
can screen many candidates, while cycle-level simulation, RTL and physical
implementation, and deployment observations permit far fewer evaluations. The
rows describe acquisition cost and practical sample budgets. They do not rank
the usefulness of the returned observations.

The measured markers ground the cycle-level and physical-design rows. FireSim
can provide cycle-exact feedback at a much higher rate
than single-server software simulation, yet it still runs below target-system
speed. A post-route macro-placement evaluation consumed eleven to fifty-five
hours of tool time per design. An AI method can spend thousands of cheap proxy
calls, but only a handful of evaluations at that physical-design cost. The
environment must expose those differences before a method chooses which tool
path to call.

@tbl-sample-cost-regimes introduced the broad cost differences among
observation sources. To make those ranges usable inside an environment, the
harness must record what each source returned and apply the same four outcome
statuses used throughout this chapter. @tbl-feedback-latency-fidelity shows how
those statuses appear at different fidelity levels.

| **Observation source** | **Cost / latency intuition** | **Environment record** | **How the status should be recorded** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Analytic proxy or learned surrogate | Milliseconds to seconds; many checks are possible. | Model and version, input domain, raw estimate, uncertainty, and declared support. | **Unsupported or out-of-scope observation** when the input lies outside declared support; otherwise **valid observation returned**, including an estimate beyond a declared tool-level bound. |
| Trace, profile, or replay | Seconds to hours depending on capture, replay, and privacy filtering. | Trace origin, workload and software version, slice rule, coverage, and redactions. | **Unsupported or out-of-scope observation** for a stale or incomplete trace; otherwise **valid observation returned** with the stated coverage. |
| Cycle-level simulation | Minutes to days depending on detail and workload. | Simulator and configuration, seed, warm-up rule, statistics, warnings, and elapsed time. | **Invalid action before execution** for a request outside the schema; **execution or tool-path failure** for a crash, timeout, or broken setup. |
| Compiler/runtime measurement | Build and profiling may take seconds to hours; target replay or tuning may take longer. | Toolchain, flags, generated code, runtime path, tests, counters, and host state. | A declared build or correctness failure is a **valid observation returned**; broken build infrastructure is an **execution or tool-path failure**. |
| RTL, synthesis, or physical feedback | Hours to weeks as timing, power, congestion, and signoff enter. | EDA stage, constraints, libraries, reports, warnings, waivers, host state, and hashes. | A timing, power, or rule violation is a **valid observation returned**; a license, queue, timeout, or tool problem is an **execution or tool-path failure**. |
| Prototype, silicon, or deployment telemetry | High setup cost; field observations may span weeks or years. | Hardware revision, rollout state, telemetry schema, workload mix, incidents, and collection gaps. | A declared regression is a **valid observation returned**; a missing or confounded signal may be an **unsupported or out-of-scope observation**. |

: **Observation sources have different costs and outcome meanings.** Representative ranges are project-dependent. The environment records enough context to separate a valid tool observation from an invalid action, broken tool path, or unsupported return. {#tbl-feedback-latency-fidelity tbl-colwidths="[19,22,32,25]"}

The last column of @tbl-feedback-latency-fidelity applies the four statuses to
concrete tool results. A cache request outside the legal range is invalid before
execution. A valid configuration that exceeds a declared bandwidth limit
returns a valid observation of that violation. A simulator crash caused by a
broken build is an execution or tool-path failure. A model output outside its
declared input domain is an unsupported or out-of-scope observation. The harness should retain
contradictory observations from two tool paths separately rather than average
away their different sources and meanings.

Tool results can also attribute a software weakness to the hardware candidate.
A candidate can look weak because the schedule, tiling, vectorization,
layout, or runtime path is poor, not because the architecture is poor. That is
a false negative created by the software side of the environment. The tool path
for a proposed vector capability must therefore include compiler and runtime
observations showing whether software can use it. The existence of an ISA
feature is insufficient.

The environment returns each observation with its source, cost, provenance,
fidelity, and tool-level outcome. Choosing which observation to request next is
a method decision developed in
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization. Determining what the returned
observation supports belongs to @sec-feedback-verification-trust.

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

## Environment Failures and Simulator Integrity

Architecture tools omit behavior outside their modeled boundary. Simulators,
analytical models, profilers, and compiler cost models encode workload choices,
warm-up rules, timing models, memory-system assumptions, compiler defaults,
branch predictor state, cache initialization, interconnect models, and sampling
choices. An AI method can search parameter corners that an architect rarely
tries and expose an unsupported state or a weakness in the wrapper.

A mapping method may enter a state in which the memory model omits contention.
A compiler autotuner may rely on a backend behavior that changes under another
target. An automated memory partitioner may find a partition that hides latency
in simulation even though a later place-and-route path reports severe
congestion. At the tool layer, the immediate requirement is precise reporting.
The simulator return is valid only for the modeled configuration and workload;
an out-of-model corner is unsupported or out of scope. The later question of
what those observations establish belongs to
@sec-feedback-verification-trust.

A completed tool call therefore does not always mean that the environment
returned a supported observation. The wrapper should detect invalid parameter
combinations before execution, preserve warnings and declared constraint
failures after execution, and mark output outside the tool's documented scope.
The harness should retain the raw output even when the method sees only a
scalar objective.

Architecture-tool integrity can also fail when a trace is poisoned, a baseline
or dependency changes without being recorded, or a log or tool result is
tampered with. Version and artifact hashes should expose those changes, while
targeted red-team checks[^fn-ml-red-teaming] should confirm that the harness
detects them before comparing runs.

A simulator-backed harness should record warm-up policy, execution- versus
trace-driven mode, random seeds, sampled regions, versioned workloads,
configuration files, and unsupported states. Tests should deliberately change
a trace, dependency, tool version, unsupported parameter, and corrupted output
so the maintainer can confirm that the harness records the correct tool-level
outcome.

[^fn-ml-red-teaming]: Here, a red-team check deliberately changes a trace, baseline, dependency, or tool record to test whether the harness detects the change before comparing outputs.

## Build, Test, and Maintain the Environment

An architect can begin with one small task. The AI method proposes actions
through a declared schema, the tools return recorded observations, and the
harness records whether an action was invalid, a valid observation returned,
the execution path failed, or the observation was unsupported. The task should
be narrow enough that its actions, observations, constraints, and tool outcomes
can be stated without private knowledge.

The steps below make that tool path usable in a reviewable architecture
comparison.

**Environment recipe.** The following steps construct a small Architecture 2.0 environment whose action rules, observations, and tool outcomes can be tested.

1. Define the task in architectural language, such as workload characterization, cache exploration, accelerator parameter search, compiler/runtime tuning, chiplet partitioning, reliability analysis, or design-review critique.
2. Choose the representation the AI method will read and write, such as a configuration file, architecture description, graph, trace record, report, RTL fragment, or test bench.
3. Define the action schema by stating which fields the AI method may change, which are read-only, and which combinations the wrapper accepts.
4. Wrap the tool path, such as a simulator, compiler, profiler, RTL flow, EDA stage, runtime system, benchmark harness, or telemetry pipeline.
5. Define the observations and their meaning, including metrics, traces, logs, warnings, errors, generated artifacts, units, aggregation, denominator, normalization, preferred direction, uncertainty, valid scope, cost, and fidelity.
6. Define four tool-level outcomes. Record an invalid action before execution, a valid observation returned including any declared constraint failure, an execution or tool-path failure, or an unsupported or out-of-scope observation.
7. Preserve raw outputs and provenance, including tool versions, seeds, workload versions, invalid actions, execution failures, unsupported observations, and the follow-up associated with each status.

Once built, the readiness test is simple. Could a second method, student, or
research group act inside the same harness and interpret its observations
without private knowledge from the original author? Could an observation from a
candidate remain understandable six months later after tool versions, workload
revisions, and scripts have changed? If the answer is no, the project may still
contain a useful wrapper, but it has not yet produced a maintainable
Architecture 2.0 environment.

These steps are sufficient for a small but useful environment. The first useful
environment for a new subfield is often narrow. A bounded workload, legal
action space, documented wrapper, and recorded outcomes let a method propose
candidates and let a reviewer reconstruct what happened to each one.

The same contract applies to a single hardware prefetcher. Its actions are
prefetch depth and pattern. A cycle-level simulator returns cheap feedback on
cache hit rate and memory bandwidth. A configuration that breaches a declared
bandwidth bound still produces a valid observation of that violation. The
environment names the workload trace, simulator version, configurations,
failed tool calls, unsupported states, and every returned metric. A later
review decides what action follows.

A change to a parser, tool default, workload version, outcome status,
or action schema can alter the comparison even when the AI method is unchanged.
Replay representative candidates after such a change, and do not combine the
old and new results until an architect has explained any difference.

::: {.callout-design-principle title="Keep tool meaning stable while methods change"}
Version legal actions, tool settings, observation schemas, and outcome statuses
together. After any change, replay representative cases before combining old
and new observations. A method comparison is meaningful only when the harness
preserves what each action and return meant.
:::

### Keeping the Environment Valid as Tools Change {#sec-environment-lifecycle}

An environment remains useful only while its workloads, legal actions, and tool
outputs still match the architecture question. If the workload is wrong, the
action space omits the important decision, the simulator hides a constraint,
an output falls outside declared support, or the logging drops invalid actions
or failed runs, an AI method may simply become faster at using a broken or stale
environment.

To preserve that link, check the parts of the environment that can go stale.

- **Workload:** The inputs, distributions, and software stack match the
  intended use.
- **Actions:** The AI method can change the right architectural variables
  without violating hidden constraints.
- **Observations:** Returned metrics identify their source, units, aggregation,
  and declared scope.
- **Feedback cost:** Cost and latency are known before the method or
  architect selects the next evaluation.
- **Provenance:** Recorded tool versions, inputs, assumptions, and outputs
  support audit. Replay additionally requires executable bindings to the
  declared environment and procedure.
- **Outcomes:** Each result uses one of four statuses: invalid action before
  execution, valid observation returned, execution or tool-path failure, or
  unsupported or out-of-scope observation.

The following checkpoint applies these requirements before a tool result is used.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Before Comparing Tool Output"}
Before comparing observations from an AI-assisted environment, confirm:

- the workload, action space, and observations still match the bounded
  architecture question;
- the record states each observation's source, units, aggregation,
  normalization, uncertainty, valid scope, and the cost and latency of another
  evaluation;
- versions, assumptions, invalid actions, and execution failures are recorded;
- each result has one of the four declared tool-level outcomes, with the
  follow-up action recorded separately for unsupported or out-of-scope
  observations.
:::

Ordinary lifecycle practice keeps those parts aligned over time. Versioning,
monitoring, drift detection, rollback, and ownership records are familiar from
MLOps[^fn-systems-mlops], benchmark governance, and reliable service operation.
Applied here, they keep an environment's workloads, tools, action schemas, and
outcome meanings from changing silently after the first paper or release.

[^fn-systems-mlops]: MLOps maintains deployed learning systems through practices such as versioning, monitoring, drift detection, rollback, and ownership records [@SculleyEtAl2015Hidden; @BeyerEtAl2016SRE]. The same practices help keep architecture harnesses comparable as their workloads and tools change.

@tbl-environment-lifecycle maps common sources of drift to the information an
architect must preserve and the response each change may require. Recording
ordinary version and workload changes prevents them from silently changing
what an observation means.

| **Drift source** | **What to record** | **Risk if ignored** | **What to do** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Workload and benchmark updates | Versions, scenarios, excluded inputs, telemetry shifts, and coverage changes. | An AI method keeps acting on a stale or unrepresentative slice. | Re-run representative cases and mark which prior observations used the old workload. |
| Tool and model changes | Simulator, compiler, EDA tool, model, prompt, policy, dependency, seed, configuration, artifact hashes, and signatures. | Results become incomparable across runs without a clear cause, or a changed dependency silently alters the tool source. | Recheck affected runs and preserve old observations. |
| Action-space changes | New knobs, deprecated knobs, invalid combinations, and permission boundaries. | A method proposes actions the environment no longer supports. | Update validity checks and record invalid-action traces. |
| Observation and outcome rules | Metric definitions, fidelity labels, warning classes, status rules, and unsupported cases. | The same output receives a different meaning or status after an interface change. | Version the schema and replay cases that exercise every outcome. |
| Harness maintenance | Maintainer, release date, compatibility policy, archived environment, and known failures. | No one repairs a stale wrapper or explains whether old runs remain comparable. | Assign a maintainer, publish the change, and retain the prior runnable release. |

: **Maintaining the environment preserves tool meaning over time.** Version workloads, tools, actions, observation schemas, outcome rules, and runnable releases so that drift does not silently change what an AI-assisted result means. {#tbl-environment-lifecycle tbl-colwidths="[18,25,25,22]"}

Each row shows how an unrecorded change can make old results incomparable.
Versioning and representative replay identify which runs need attention before
old and new observations are combined.

## Open Research Questions

Five parts of making this contract executable remain difficult across real
tool flows.

1. **What is the smallest environment contract that lets an AI method act
without private knowledge?** @tbl-environment-contract identifies common
fields, but their required detail varies across simulators, compilers,
commercial EDA flows, and deployment systems. Research should identify which
omissions actually cause an independent method to take invalid actions,
mislabel a tool return, or produce an observation another architect cannot
interpret.

2. **Can AI infer a candidate environment contract from legacy tool traces
without treating accidental behavior as a rule?** Legacy EDA flows and
simulators expose warnings, timeouts, partial artifacts, and unstructured logs
rather than clean APIs. Inferred action schemas and outcome rules must capture
stable tool behavior while leaving uncertain cases visible for an architect to
correct.

3. **How should ambiguous outcomes be attributed after an AI-generated action?**
Invalid actions, valid observations of tool-level constraint failures,
execution failures, and unsupported outputs require different records. A
timeout, for example, could reflect an extreme but legal candidate or unstable
infrastructure. The classification must preserve that ambiguity without
silently blaming the candidate or the tool path.

4. **How can AI use a physical-tool observation to constrain a cheaper
environment without erasing its scope?** Routing congestion, IR drop, timing
violations, and design-rule failures may reveal limits that a simulator does
not represent. Transferring a redacted constraint into a cheaper environment
could avoid repeated invalid regions, but the constraint may not hold across
workloads, floorplans, or technology nodes. @tbl-eda-stage-contract shows the
stages that the transferred observation must continue to identify.

5. **Which AI-assisted runs must be replayed after an environment changes?** A
new workload, simulator, model, prompt, dependency, action schema, or outcome
rule can alter some earlier observations without changing all of them. A useful
system would trace each environment change to the actions and observations it
can alter, then identify the smallest representative replay set.

## Conclusion

An architecture environment makes a tool flow usable by an AI method and
reviewable by another architect. It tells the method what it may change and
tells the architect how to interpret what the tool returns. That requires the
workload and fixed context, action schema, observation meaning, cost and
fidelity, and provenance. The four tool-level outcomes are invalid action
before execution, valid observation returned, execution or tool-path failure,
and unsupported or out-of-scope observation. A declared constraint failure
reported by a completed tool path remains a valid observation. The environment
does not decide what that observation proves or who may authorize the next
architecture decision.

The contract remains reliable only while the workload, permitted actions,
tools, and observation meanings still match the architecture question. When
one changes, versioning and provenance show which results need to be rechecked
and make representative runs replayable. A one-time wrapper may support one
experiment. A maintained environment lets the next architect determine
whether the harness still answers the same question.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** An environment specifies legal actions, observation meaning, provenance, acquisition cost, and four tool-level outcomes so another method can act in the same harness.
- **Reader test:** Could another method act in the same harness, and could another architect interpret its results, without private knowledge from the original author?
- **Up next:** Once the environment defines what action and feedback mean, @sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization can match method roles to the available feedback.
:::
